2023 · NAACL · Benchmarks & Evals

AGIEval: A Human-Centric Benchmark for Evaluating Foundation Models

Microsoft repurposes 20 official human admission and qualification exams — the Gaokao, SAT, LSAT, GRE/GMAT, the Chinese bar — into a released, bilingual benchmark of 8,062 objective questions, then asks whether GPT-4 actually beats the humans who sit them.

Wanjun Zhong, Ruixiang Cui, Yiduo Guo, Yaobo Liang, Shuai Lu, Yanlin Wang, Amin Saied, Weizhu Chen, Nan Duan · Microsoft
venue NAACL citations 847 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

The benchmarks that defined a decade of NLP — GLUE, SuperGLUE, SQuAD, SNLI — are artificially curated datasets built to probe a single machine skill (answer extraction, paraphrase, entailment). They test what is convenient to label, not the complex, multi-step reasoning humans are actually tested on. As foundation models started clearing them, the question shifted from "can the model do this narrow task?" to "how does it stack up against a person?"

MMLU pushed in the human-centric direction by scraping subject-matter questions from the web, but AGIEval's authors argue it has two soft spots: its provenance is opaque (it is unclear whether the items come from professional, high-stakes sources), and it is English-only. Meanwhile the splashiest human-comparison numbers of 2023 came from the GPT-4 technical report, which advertised bar-exam and SAT scores — but the exam sets, the model outputs, and the scoring method were never released, so no one outside OpenAI could reproduce, audit, or extend the comparison.

AGIEval's bet is that the most meaningful yardstick for a "general" model is the same set of official, high-standard exams society already uses to gate humans into universities and professions — and that such a benchmark only has scientific value if the data, the model outputs, and an automatic metric are all public and reproducible.

// how it works

The method

AGIEval is a collection, not a model. The authors assemble 20 tasks / 8,062 questions from real exams taken by millions annually: the Chinese College Entrance Exam (Gaokao, 8 subjects from history to physics; ~12M test-takers/yr), the SAT (English + Math), GRE/GMAT-style algebra (via AQuA-RAT), the LSAT (analytical, logical, and reading-comprehension sections from 1991–2016), the Chinese lawyer qualification exam (JEC-QA), the national civil-service exam (LogiQA), and high-school math competitions AMC/AIME (via the MATH dataset). The benchmark is deliberately bilingual (English + Chinese).

Crucially, only objective questions survive: multiple-choice and fill-in-the-blank items are kept; all subjective/essay questions are removed so scoring can be automatic and consistent. Multiple-choice is graded by accuracy; fill-in-the-blank by Exact Match (and F1). As a yardstick, each task carries an average (50th-percentile) and top (top-1%, or top-10% for the bar) human score. For MATH, LogiQA and JEC-QA these come from the source publications; for SAT, LSAT, Gaokao and GRE/GMAT they are estimated by rescaling raw test-taker scores onto a 0–100 scale:

human % = test-taker raw scoreexam full marks × 100 // avg = 50th-percentile taker, top = top 1% (top 10% for the bar). A percentile→percent rescaling — not measured accuracy on the objective subset. This estimate is load-bearing for the headline claim.

Every model is run under four protocols — zero-shot and few-shot (5 in-context examples), each with and without Chain-of-Thought. CoT is two-step: first prompt the model with "Let's think step by step" to produce an explanation, then feed that explanation back to extract the final answer. All generations use temperature 0 (greedy decoding) up to 2,048 tokens via the Azure OpenAI APIs.

The models on trial are a 2023 snapshot: three closed OpenAI models — GPT-4, ChatGPT, and Text-Davinci-003 (GPT-3.5) — plus one open model, Vicuna-13B, a LLaMA fine-tune that claimed ~90% of ChatGPT quality on community leaderboards.

// what they found

The results

GPT-4 dominates the field. Under zero-shot CoT its all-task average is 58.4, well ahead of ChatGPT (43.2) and Text-Davinci-003 (37.4); few-shot CoT nudges it to 61.3. On individual exams the numbers are striking: GPT-4 hits 95.0% on SAT-Math, 92.5% on Gaokao-English, and ~85% on the LSAT's reading-comprehension and logical-reasoning sections — clearing the average-human line on SAT, LSAT, and math competitions.

But the radar chart hides the asterisks. GPT-4's 58.4 average sits below the 67 average-human line across all tasks, and it stays well inside the top-1% human envelope everywhere. The wins are concentrated in verbal recall and standard math; the losses are in genuine multi-step reasoning.

Task (zero-shot CoT)GPT-4Avg humanTop human
SAT-Math95.06694
Gaokao-English92.56991
LSAT — reading comp.85.15691
LSAT — logical reas.80.65691
MATH (AMC/AIME)47.74090
Gaokao-physics45.57194
LSAT — analytical reas.34.45691
Gaokao-Math (fill-in)15.37396
All-task average58.46791

Three findings travel beyond GPT-4. First, complex reasoning is the wall: every model — GPT-4 included — struggles on MATH, LSAT analytical reasoning, Gaokao physics, and Gaokao fill-in math. Second, few-shot barely beats zero-shot (zero-shot CoT 58.4 vs few-shot CoT 61.3 for GPT-4) — a sharp reversal from the original GPT-3, where in-context examples mattered enormously, which the authors credit to instruction tuning. Third, CoT is double-edged: it lifts English math (MATH, AQuA, SAT-Math) but degrades several other tasks, and it is language-sensitive — helping LogiQA-English while hurting the Chinese version.

The qualitative audit — human experts scoring 100 wrong ChatGPT answers per task along four axes — localizes the failure. Understanding is consistently strong; reasoning and calculation are the weak links, with the model ignoring premise conditions, confusing sufficient vs. necessary conditions, fumbling counterfactuals, and silently substituting one concept for a similar one.

Finally, the open model gets a reality check: despite Vicuna-13B's leaderboard reputation, it lands in the 20s–30s on most AGIEval tasks (e.g. 26.4 on LogiQA-English zero-shot, 30.5 on SAT-Math), a wide gap to the closed models that underscores how challenging these exams remain for open systems.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

Contamination is the elephant in the room — and it goes unmeasured. Every source AGIEval draws on (Gaokao, SAT, LSAT items from 1991–2016, AMC/AIME, JEC-QA, LogiQA) is public, heavily reposted, and almost certainly inside GPT-4's pretraining corpus. The paper runs no contamination analysis — no n-gram overlap, no canary string, no held-out probe. GPT-4's 95% on SAT-Math is exactly the kind of widely-circulated content most at risk of memorization, and the GPT-4 technical report itself flagged exam contamination as a concern. AGIEval inherits the problem without quantifying it, so on the headline tasks you cannot separate capability from recall.

The human baseline is a back-of-envelope estimate, yet the headline depends on it. For SAT, LSAT, Gaokao and GRE/GMAT the authors rescale percentile cut-offs (50th, 1st) to a 0–100 scale — a percentile→percent conversion that does not correspond to accuracy on these specific objective subsets. "GPT-4 beats the average human" therefore rests on an estimated, scale-mismatched line. And the framing buries the most honest number: on the benchmark's own all-task average GPT-4 (58.4) trails the average human (67) — the per-exam wins are real but selectively spotlit by the radar chart.

Only the objective skeleton is tested. Dropping all subjective questions makes scoring automatic but reduces the bar exam, GMAT writing and Gaokao essays to their multiple-choice husk. These items are "human-centric," but they measure recognition and short-answer, not the open-ended argumentation the real exams gate on. There is also a quiet methodological mismatch: ChatGPT's few-shot runs use the completion API while GPT-4 only had the chat API, so the two models' few-shot numbers are not strictly comparable. And answer parsing looks brittle — SAT-English-without-passage collapses to 25.2 under zero-shot CoT from 51.0 zero-shot, a 26-point drop that reads more like a formatting/parsing artifact than a reasoning result, with no parser error rate reported.

baselines

Three OpenAI models + one open model (Vicuna-13B) — a 2023 snapshot with no PaLM/Claude/LLaMA-2. Human "average/top" lines are rescaled percentile estimates, not measured per-task accuracy.

contamination

Untested. Every exam source is public and reposted; SAT/LSAT/Gaokao items are prime pretraining fodder, and no overlap or decontamination check is run.

what's missed

Subjective sections dropped entirely; the all-task average (GPT-4 58.4 vs human 67) is below human even as per-exam wins are headlined; ChatGPT vs GPT-4 few-shot uses different APIs.

would raise confidence

An n-gram/canary contamination audit, measured human accuracies on the exact objective subsets, a reported answer-parser error rate, and a refresh across modern and open models.

// bottom line

AGIEval's durable contribution is the released, reproducible, bilingual exam corpus and full model outputs — a genuine upgrade over the opaque exam numbers in vendor model cards. The "GPT-4 beats humans" headline is the least defensible part: it leans on an estimated human baseline and an unaudited contamination risk, and quietly omits that GPT-4 trails the average human on the benchmark's own all-task average. Cite it for the dataset and the capability breakdown, not for the leaderboard verdict.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. AGIEval turns 20 official human exams — Gaokao, SAT, LSAT, GRE/GMAT, the Chinese bar, civil service, AMC/AIME — into 8,062 bilingual objective questions, a reproducible alternative to the opaque exam scores in model cards (data + model outputs released).
  2. GPT-4 is genuinely strong on verbal and standard-math items (95% SAT-Math, 92.5% Gaokao-English, ~85% LSAT reading/logic) but collapses on multi-step reasoning (LSAT analytical 34%, physics 46%, Gaokao fill-in math 15%) — and its 58.4 all-task average trails the ~67 average-human line.
  3. By 2023, zero-shot ≈ few-shot for instruction-tuned LLMs (a reversal of GPT-3), and CoT is a double-edged tool: it lifts English math but degrades several tasks and is language-sensitive.
  4. The "beats humans" claim is the weakest link — public-exam contamination is untested and the human baseline is a rescaled percentile estimate; treat the dataset and the four-axis capability audit as the contribution, the headline as marketing.
